body.content: Deciding where to live is never easy. You have to think about each location's lifestyle, weather, career, schools, etc. But at least we have an accessible repository of that information now. I can't imagine how hard it was to decide where to live during the Age of Exploration.

With maritime technology finally (though barely) at a requisite state for transatlantic travel, there was an explosion of colonial pursuits. The Dutch, Portuguese, Spanish, English, and even the Italians and Belgians all wanted a piece of the pie. Though one problem remained: who was to inhabit these newly established colonies? After all, planting a flag only gets you so far. It's the endurance of a population, people who work to extract and create value, and to expand territory, that yields true wealth. Knowing this, colonial powers constructed myriad tales to excite the populace about moving to a colony. The pitch is tough – who wants to brave months (often indefinite periods of time) of horrid weather and food confined to a tiny cabin on a boat that may or may not break for the slim chance of acquiring godly wealth and the high chance of contracting a deadly disease? The negatives weren't mentioned, and the positives were highly distorted, not to mention the opportunity cost wasn't exactly high. Disease, famine, and persecution plagued Europe at the time, and clearly pushed many to question how bad a chance at a new life could possibly be.

Scores of Europeans embarked on journeys to the Americas, East Indies, Africa, and India in pursuit of a better life and with the promise of fortune. Many found the former, almost none found the latter. And, centuries later, many of their descendants still live and define these former colonies. However, there's immense variance in the quality of life and opportunity across the colonies. There's a power law distribution, where a small number of colonies are wealthy–even wealthier than their establishing countries–and most are poor, middle-income at best.

Take Singapore (where I'm currently en route). It's a former British colony turned Malaysian territory turned exiled Malaysian territory. It has no endowed advantages, and yet it's become one of the wealthiest countries in the world, and arguably the most technologically advanced. It would've been downright foolish to be an Englishman (or Chinese or Indian) who chose to move their family to Singapore during colonial times. However, as long as you made a few good decisions, you'd almost certainly be extremely well off living in one of the most prosperous countries in the world.

On the other hand, take Venezuela. It enjoys a prime perch at the top of South America, ideally situated for trade with North America, the rest of South America, and transatlantic trade with Europe and Africa. Moreover, it's the most oil-rich country in the world. It would've been a great bet as a Spaniard to move to Venezuela. Yet, somehow, it's relatively low-income, suffers from inordinate amounts of corruption, and is ruled by a totalitarian dictator. Much of the same can be said of every South American colony (save for Costa Rica and Chile, which have their own problems).

America is the prototypical example. Its genesis was an improbable act (defeating the world's most powerful army), and it has since actualized much of the gargantuan potential it has. In fact, it seems the safest bet by far would've been to be an Englander who moved to a colony in a location unfettered by an established local population. The United States, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand are all high-income, safe, and enjoy super high (higher than the U.K.) standards of living. On the other hand, perhaps the worst bet would've been to be an Englishman moving to a colony with an established population: Nigeria, South Africa, and India aren't exactly high on the world's best places to live list.

I can only imagine how much nuance I'm missing. But the point is that emigres of the time were playing the world's biggest roulette game. Some ended up living in the wealthiest countries in the world; others were far worse than had they simply stayed put. I suppose that a good way to think about risk is that it usually has a long-tailed distribution skewed FAR right. The question is how you maximize your odds of ending up in Singapore or America.
This morning, I walked past a SET store with an advertisement:
The brand everyone was wearing in your pilates class this morning.

Immediately, I was struck by how similar everyone's purchasing habits seem to be these days. A few idea hops later, I started to connect the dots between similarity in various domains, and registered that people's lives are converging on buying the same clothes, consuming the same media, eating at the same restaurants + {...} + having the same, perfectly fungible lives.

It isn't profound to point out that as technology has progressed, so too has the world's connectedness and similarity. It's simply more efficient. McDonald's, which started as a Southern Californian experiment, is now consumed in almost every country in the world, and is the de facto model for quick, cheap meals. Ditto for culture (Hollywood movies and music), clothes (H&M, Nike, etc.), and almost everything else you can think of.

At USC, students wear the same brands (Aviator Nation, Golden Goose, Alo), buy the same bags and accessories (Goyard Bags, Stanley Cups, Cartier/Van Cleef bracelets), and are transfixed by the same media (Love Island). I've even seen posts about the new grad real estate market in NYC standardizing for studio apartments with white walls and exposed brick.

Perhaps the most troubling domain is food. SYSCO, of course, is the canonical example. It's monopolized ingredient supply chains for restaurants (mostly in the middle/bottom 50%) across the country, and even the types of dishes that are served. Nowadays, you'd be hard-pressed to find a restaurant sourcing local ingredients and showcasing unique, regional dishes.

There's a similar (ish) situation in software. Figma has a product called FigJam, which is essentially Figma with tons of design constraints; irrespective of who's using FigJam, the result will look more or less the same. And, on average, the designs are clean, polished, and functional. But the best digital designs in the world are all painted on the main Figma canvas, a place that's far less constrained and standardized. Without guardrails to bump into, you're able to color outside the lines and discover better art than you would've otherwise.

When everyone shares the same life, you're not actually living at all; life is defined by ambiguity and agency, and traipsing through a world optimized for efficiency lacks both. Luckily for us, the world is an enormous place and still hyper diverse in almost every domain. The challenge remains in creating variety within smaller bubbles, but striking the right balance (everyone catching up on the same TV shows while having wildly diverging ambitions for the future and tastes in music) is a prize worth fighting for.

Maps are something I've always been interested in. My best guess as to why is their ability to condense oodles of important, occasionally life-saving information into a beautiful illustration. In many ways, maps are the most effective information design product humanity has. Antique maps are likely the best example of the above phenomena, but in high school, I found myself spending hours on Google Maps to see what an isolated town in San Carlos de Bariloche looked like, MSN maps to see how temperature varied across the globe, and Zoom.Earth to see how full Felt Lake was in different years.

It's not an exaggeration to say maps are on my mind more than the average person. That's why, when my family drove down to L.A. over Thanksgiving Break to visit me, I was able to condense the clouds of an idea around transit (and life in general) into something tangible.

Santa Monica is easily my most traveled-to location while in school. It provides the canonical L.A. experience–beaches, the iconic pier, great restaurants–and more importantly, can be reached in an hour via the E Line train that has two stops on campus. And, because you can take a (free) train to get there, it's possible to do "work" on the way there and back: reading and answering emails, reading a book, and occasionally taking a music tech final.

Even though there are many other nice–or at least interesting–locations in the city (Malibu, Manhattan Beach, Beverly Hills, West Hollywood, Pasadena, etc.), I hardly travel elsewhere unless I'm with a car-owning friend. What's even more interesting is that lots of these alternative locations are actually geographically closer to campus than Santa Monica.

For a while, I attributed the conglomeration of my escapades to public transit availability. During Thanksgiving break, however, when my parents drove down and we spent hours driving from our Airbnb in WeHo to campus, to Santa Monica, to Malibu, and to Palos Verdes, I realized there was more going for the USC-Santa Monica route than I initially gave it credit for. From campus, you can drive directly onto the highway, and if there's no traffic, get to the beach in 15 or 20 minutes. In contrast, it took us almost an hour to drive from the village to the temporary Hollywood home. The streets weren't necessarily in gridlock, but we faced traffic light after traffic light that meaningfully hindered our ability to make progress. Not only that, but it made the entire journey incredibly fuel-inefficient. This is one of a million examples I can imagine where distance and ease/effort of travel don't have a proportional relationship.

This, naturally, applies well to traveling. Time and energy are limited in college, and it's a great feeling to be efficient when spending time on non-leisure travel. At school, this means spending time going to Santa Monica and biking around instead of Hollywood or Pasadena. And beyond, it's taking the Southwest flight out of Burbank instead of LAX to avoid the god-awful LAXit bus, or choosing to visit my friend in Sydney because there are fewer steps involved than traveling to Ithaca.

Perhaps more importantly, it can be broadly applied to almost any pursuit in life. I'm not totally sure about other people, but there are a number of different life paths that are highly compelling to me. I could fade everything to move to Switzerland and become a ski instructor, try and become an elite athlete and train relentlessly to make it to the next Olympics, or become world-class at product building and create a billion-dollar company… And, on paper, it might seem harder (more distance) for me to achieve (travel to) than others. However, once you factor in the unique advantages in traveling to your destination–the metro E line, direct flights, and point-to-point highways–it becomes clear that not all equal distances are created equally. This is a highly imperfect articulation of an idea I've had for a long time (tailwinds) and one that's popped up recently (maps for time/effort instead of distance).
